summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is the antiderivative of f(x) = 5x^4?

Using the power rule for integration: integral of 5x^4 dx = 5(x^5/5) + C = x^5 + C. You can verify by differentiating: d/dx(x^5) = 5x^4.

What is the integral of 1/x^3 dx?

Rewrite 1/x^3 = x^(-3). Using the power rule: integral of x^(-3) dx = x^(-2)/(-2) + C = -1/(2x^2) + C.

What is the integral of e^(3x) dx?

Using the rule for exponentials with a linear argument: integral of e^(ax) dx = (1/a)e^(ax) + C. Here a = 3, so the answer is (1/3)e^(3x) + C.

What is the integral of sec^2(x) dx?

Since d/dx[tan(x)] = sec^2(x), the antiderivative of sec^2(x) is tan(x) + C.

What is the antiderivative of f(x) = 0?

The antiderivative of 0 is any constant C, because the derivative of any constant is 0. This illustrates why the constant of integration is always included.

What is an antiderivative of a function?

An antiderivative of f(x) is any function F(x) such that F'(x) = f(x). Antidifferentiation is the reverse process of differentiation.

What is the 'power rule for integration'?

The power rule for integration states: integral of x^n dx = x^(n+1)/(n+1) + C, provided n is not -1. When n = -1, the integral is ln|x| + C instead.

What is an 'indefinite integral'?

An indefinite integral represents the family of all antiderivatives of a function. It has no limits of integration and always includes the constant C to represent the entire family F(x) + C.

What is a 'particular antiderivative'?

A particular antiderivative is one specific member of the family F(x) + C, determined by an initial condition such as F(a) = b. This pins down the exact value of C.

What is meant by 'initial condition' when finding antiderivatives?

An initial condition like F(0) = 5 provides enough information to determine the constant C in the general antiderivative F(x) + C. Substituting the known values and solving for C gives the particular solution.

What is the 'sum and difference rule' for indefinite integrals?

The sum/difference rule states: integral of [f(x) +/- g(x)] dx = integral of f(x) dx +/- integral of g(x) dx. This allows us to integrate polynomials and sums term by term.

A population grows at a rate of P'(t) = 100 + 20t individuals per year. If the initial population is 5000, what is the population after 10 years?

P(t) = integral of (100 + 20t) dt = 100t + 10t^2 + C. P(0) = C = 5000. P(10) = 1000 + 1000 + 5000 = 7000 individuals.

An object's velocity is v(t) = 3 cos(t) m/s and its initial position is s(0) = 2 meters. What is the position function?

s(t) = integral of 3 cos(t) dt = 3 sin(t) + C. Using s(0) = 2: 3 sin(0) + C = 2, so C = 2. Therefore s(t) = 3 sin(t) + 2.

A heating element warms a room at rate T'(t) = 8/(t+1) degrees per hour. If the room starts at 60 degrees, what is the temperature after 3 hours?

T(t) = integral of 8/(t+1) dt = 8 ln|t+1| + C. T(0) = 8 ln(1) + C = C = 60. T(3) = 8 ln(4) + 60 = 60 + 8 ln(4) degrees.
